policy_statement: Valorise genetic material of agricultural and forestry varieties and species to reduce susceptibility to emerging diseases and pests.
mechanism_description: Improving the genetic resilience of crops and forest species reduces vulnerability to emerging pests and diseases, strengthening climate adaptation capacity in managed and natural landscapes.
typology_evidence_quote: "reduce susceptibility to emerging diseases and pests"
primary_causal_pathway: Climate change–shifted pest/disease pressure → genetically resilient crops/trees → ↓ sensitivity/impact (yield loss, mortality) → ↑ resilience of managed/natural landscapes
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: breeding, selecting, conserving, and deploying crop varieties and forest reproductive material with enhanced pest/disease resistance (including seed sourcing and species/variety choice in planting programs).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: improved host genetic resistance/tolerance reduces infection rates, slows pathogen/pest spread, and lowers mortality/defoliation under conditions that favor outbreaks (warmer temperatures, altered precipitation, new pest ranges).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: reduced vulnerability and damages (more stable yields, forest health, and continuity of ecosystem services) under climate change, thereby increasing adaptive capacity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced climate-driven yield and ecosystem losses by lowering exposure/sensitivity to emerging pests and diseases.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: addresses a climate hazard pathway (changing pest/disease regimes) by reducing system sensitivity through genetic resilience, leading to lower damages—an adaptation effect.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while healthier forests/crops can have secondary mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., avoided replanting inputs, maintained carbon stocks), the mechanism’s direct intent and largest effect is risk reduction and resilience to climate-amplified biological threats.  
3) Edge cases applied: if implemented mainly to increase productivity absent a climate-risk framing, it could resemble resource efficiency/agronomic improvement; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets emerging pests/diseases under climate change, which is adaptation-dominant.  
4) Applicability across all instances: any policy that improves genetic resilience to pests/diseases functions by lowering vulnerability to climate-shifted biotic stressors, so Adaptation remains the primary category consistently.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment: any secondary mitigation or nature-based co-benefits (e.g., avoided forest dieback maintaining carbon sinks) must be assessed at the individual policy level and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential overlap with Mitigation when framed as preventing forest mortality to maintain carbon stocks, or with Resource Efficiency when primarily aimed at yield gains; classification stays Adaptation because the dominant mechanism is reducing climate-related pest/disease vulnerability.
key_indicators: "Valorisation of the genetic material"; "agricultural and forestry varieties and species"; "reduce susceptibility"; "emerging diseases and pests"
co_benefits: Biodiversity/genetic conservation; improved productivity and product quality; reduced pesticide use and associated environmental/health benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via incentive-program (subsidies for resistant varieties) or infrastructure-investment (gene banks/R&D facilities), but the text itself does not name funding, standards, or mandates—so it is best treated as a strategy-level commitment.
